According to the Ḥanafī school of thought, which you follow, it's not simply a matter of working at a bank being automatically permissible or impermissible. The key issue is whether your role involves directly handling or facilitating interest (riba). Here's how classical jurists and contemporary fatwas break it down: ✅ When it's permissible: Roles with no direct engagement in interest-based work—like working in marketing, IT support, call centers (when not advising on interest loans), cleaning, security—can be deemed allowed (mubah) or at least disliked but permissible (makruh) because your job doesn’t assist with riba transactions. IslamQA explains that jobs “without direct involvement whatsoever with interest-based transactions… would be allowable,” though “superior to avoid” islamqa.org +7 islamqa.org +7 forum.islamicfinanceguru.com +7 . SeekersGuidance similarly confirms that providing general marketing or software support is fine, so long as it's not aimed at enabling riba-based services seekersguidance.org +1 seekersguidance.org +1 . ❌ When it's impermissible: If your work directly involves interest transactions—like processing loans, drafting interest-based contracts, cash handling linked to interest, advising clients on financing—then it's considered haram because you are helping facilitate riba, which Islam strictly forbids en.wikipedia.org +12 islamqa.info +12 islam.stackexchange.com +12 . 🏦 How this applies to HBL’s marketing department: Marketing is generally about promoting services and crafting campaigns—not calculating interest or facilitating loans. In a conventional bank like HBL, as long as your job is purely marketing communications, you aren’t handling interest directly. Thus: Under Ḥanafī principles, your role would be considered permissible, since the majority of your salary comes indirectly—and your tasks are free from riba-based operations islamqa.org +6 islam.stackexchange.com +6 islamqa.info +6 . ⚖️ Practical considerations: Ensure you steer clear of any tasks involving interest calculations, loan packaging, or eligibility advising. If your marketing campaigns promote credit or financing products, especially with interest, consult your local scholar or Islamic financial advisor to determine if participation in messaging crosses an ethical or legal threshold. Muslims in similar roles often view promotional campaigns as involving general communication, which is allowed so long as it does not support the sin of riba. 🌟 Summary: Marketing roles at HBL are generally permissible under the Ḥanafī school, provided you’re not involved in direct riba operations. That said, always remain cautious and consult your local scholar if your role begins to support interest-based products more directly.
As long as it's not directly involved or directly increasing the use, it can be permissible or disliked but not haram

You have 3 options here: 1) You take it and continue because ones in banking sector end up with good benefits. That’s the weakest form of imaan. 2) You take it whilst actively keep searching for other opportunities. But even when you are working there, you work with the mindset that you’re only working to learn the work, so you could someday use it to promote religion somehow. That would be permissible, given your intention is right and the heart is in right place. However still not the ideal case. 3) You reject the offer and keep searching for opportunities. You stay patient, and have firm believe in Allah that he is testing you here, and will reward you with a much better opportunity in future. This is a sign of Tawakkul and a strongest form of imaan.
If you’re concerned with permissibility, In’Sha’Allah (2) shall suffice for you
But I guarantee you that you wouldn’t even regret choosing (3)

Sophomore year ke baad I had the opportunity to intern at Bank for 30K per month, or intern at TCS for 10K per month. Bank Opportunity was better in terms of name and monetary benefit both. But choosing TCS eventually ended up working wonders for me, which you all did see yourself
So if you choose (2), don’t worry about permissibility by keeping your intentions right If you’re choosing (3), don’t worry about your career trajectory.

Here’s your story, Maaz ! Maaz lived a life sculpted by the rhythmic clang of the blacksmith's hammer. His father, a renowned craftsman in their small village nestled beside a whispering river, had taught him the ancient art. The scent of coal smoke and hot iron clung to Maaz like a second skin, a comforting familiarity. He wasn't just shaping metal; he was shaping stories, each piece a miniature narrative forged in the heart of the fire. His hands, calloused and strong, moved with a grace that belied their rough exterior. He had a quiet strength, a reserved nature that masked a deep well of emotion. Durriya, on the other hand, was a whirlwind. A vibrant splash of color in their otherwise muted village, she was known for her laughter that echoed through the marketplace, her vibrant saris, and her uncanny ability to coax stories from the most reticent souls. She was a storyteller herself, weaving tales of ancient kings and mythical creatures, her words painting vivid pictures in the minds of her listeners. Maaz, usually lost in his own world of hammer blows and sparks, found himself drawn to her energy, her life a stark contrast to his own deliberate pace. Their first meeting was accidental, a collision near the river where Maaz was collecting water. A basket of freshly picked mangoes tumbled from Durriya's hands, scattering their juicy bounty across the dusty ground. He helped her gather them, their fingers brushing lightly, a silent spark igniting between them. He noticed the intricate henna designs on her hands, a testament to her artistry, a mirror to his own skill with metal. Their connection deepened slowly, like the careful layering of metal in a complex piece of art. He would give her small, intricately crafted gifts – a tiny silver bird, a delicate bracelet forged from moonlight-hued metal. She, in turn, would share her stories, her voice a soothing melody that eased the tension in his shoulders after a long day at the forge. He found himself listening, truly listening, a skill honed not in the clang of the forge, but in the gentle cadence of her words. Their relationship was a tapestry woven from contrasting threads: his quiet contemplation and her exuberant spirit, his tangible creations and her ethereal narratives. He admired her passion, her ability to connect with people, and she, in turn, was captivated by his quiet strength, the artistry in his every movement, the depth of emotion hidden beneath his reserved demeanor. Their love story, like his finest creations, was forged in the heat of their individual personalities, tempered by time, and ultimately emerged as something beautiful, something enduring. A masterpiece.
